network.overall_strategy: This network is literature-constrained around the central observation that prebiotic asparagine is experimentally detectable by several routes but is chemically unstable and often converts to aspartate. The design therefore uses only literature-grounded direct formation routes to asparagine where available (cyanoacetylene chemistry, cyanosulfidic aminonitrile hydrolysis, spark discharge, contact glow discharge) and treats aspartate-centered hydrothermal/protometabolic chemistry primarily as convergent precursor supply rather than claiming solved amidation. Environmental interconnection is achieved by generating carbon skeletons and alpha-keto acids in hydrothermal settings, feeding these into surface cyanosulfidic and Strecker-like networks, and then allowing transient biochemical trapping by peptide incorporation. The network contains 10 target-reaching pathways, but several explicitly share late-stage direct-formation reactions because the literature supports only a limited number of experimentally demonstrated asparagine-forming chemistries. Unsupported direct amidation of aspartate is not asserted as an established route; where included, it is clearly marked speculative and used only to connect literature-identified open problems.

molecules.common_name: thioformamide precursor pool / reduced sulfur feed

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 to C1 reduced carbon on iron-sulfur phases.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed Fe(Ni)S-related hydrothermal systems reduce CO2 with H2 to formate under vent-like conditions; greigite and mackinawite are required hydrothermal mineral analogues in the present network.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal carbon fixation to acetate and pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal, anoxic; vent-generated pH gradient

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise reductive carbon fixation on iron-rich minerals generates C2 and C3 carboxylates from reduced carbon pools.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported hydrothermal formation of acetate and pyruvate from CO2/H2 on Fe-bearing minerals; this step provides literature-supported hydrothermal precursor supply rather than direct asparagine formation.

reactions.name: FeS/FeS2-mediated reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Surface-assisted reductive amination of alpha-keto acid to alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Huber & Wächtershäuser (2003) demonstrated FeS-based reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids; Barge et al. (2019) further supported mineral-mediated pyruvate-to-alanine conversion, though oxaloacetate decarboxylation remained problematic.

reactions.name: Photoredox cyanosulfidic generation of cyanoacetylene from HCN and acetylene
agents: sulfide minerals (molybdenite/stibnite proxy for sulfidic setting)

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven Cu photoredox in sulfidic phosphate buffer on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: anoxic, phosphate-buffered, UV irradiated, H2S present

reactions.mechanism: Cyanosulfidic oxidative cross-coupling under UV photoredox produces cyanoacetylene from cyanide feedstock and acetylene.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) established cyanosulfidic protometabolism using HCN, H2S, UV, and Cu photoredox at about 35 degrees C; the network retains required surface mineral agents while following the reported environmental regime.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of cyanoacetylene to cyanoacetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Partial hydration/hydrolysis of the activated nitrile-alkyne system yields beta-functionalized aldehyde precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966) proposed cyanoacetaldehyde formation from cyanoacetylene hydrolysis in aqueous prebiotic chemistry leading toward asparagine, aspartate, and cytosine.

reactions.name: Cyanoacetaldehyde Strecker-type conversion to four-carbon aminonitrile precursor
reactions.mechanism: Addition of ammonia and cyanide to cyanoacetaldehyde forms a C4 alpha-aminonitrile/dinitrile precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966) and Miller & Van Trump (1981) support Strecker-type access from cyanoacetaldehyde to asparagine/aspartate precursors; selective hydrolysis remains the key branchpoint.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic conversion of cyanoacetylene to maleonitrile and alpha-aminonitrile precursor

conditions.catalyst: UV/Cu photoredox cyanosulfidic system in phosphate buffer
conditions.other: anoxic, alkaline, excess HCN and NH3

reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetylene is funneled through maleonitrile and related nitrile intermediates to the identified alpha-aminonitrile precursor of asparagine/aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) explicitly identified maleonitrile and alpha-aminonitrile compound 49 as precursors to asparagine and aspartate under cyanosulfidic photoredox conditions.

reactions.name: Partial hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitrile to asparagine

conditions.other: anoxic aqueous pool; stopping at amide is kinetically challenging

reactions.mechanism: Selective partial hydrolysis of nitrile functionality yields side-chain amide rather than full carboxylate.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966) and Patel et al. (2015) both place selective partial hydrolysis of a nitrile precursor at the branch to asparagine; the literature emphasizes this selectivity as a major unresolved control issue.

reactions.name: Full hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitrile to aspartate

conditions.other: anoxic; greater extent of hydrolysis than rxn_008

reactions.mechanism: Further nitrile hydrolysis drives amide/carboxylate formation to aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966) and Patel et al. (2015) indicate the same aminonitrile precursor can yield aspartate upon more complete hydrolysis; this is the thermodynamically favored branch.

reactions.name: Spark-discharge atmospheric synthesis and non-hydrolytic detection of asparagine

conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge with subsequent condensate capture
conditions.other: reducing gas discharge condensate analyzed without acid hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge over reduced gas mixtures generates a complex product mixture in which free asparagine is preserved only if analysis avoids acid hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Jiang et al. (2014) reported asparagine as the highest-abundance amino acid in spark discharge products when LC-MS/MS analysis avoided destructive acid hydrolysis; this is direct experimental evidence for prebiotic asparagine formation.

reactions.name: Contact glow discharge conversion of alanine and formamide to asparagine

conditions.other: alanine 100 mM, formamide 200 mM, volcanic-lightning analogue

reactions.mechanism: Plasma-driven oxidation/rearrangement/amidation chemistry in formamide-rich solution converts alanine-containing mixtures to asparagine.
reactions.reasoning: Munegumi (2014) reported 3.0% yield of asparagine from alanine plus formamide under contact glow discharge electrolysis, providing one of the few quantified abiotic asparagine syntheses.

reactions.name: HCN hydration to formamide under UV-active surface conditions
reactions.mechanism: Hydration and concentration of HCN-derived carbon-nitrogen species yields formamide-rich reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research summary notes UV photochemistry from HCN and water to formamide as part of prebiotic surface chemistry; this connecting step is added conservatively to link HCN feedstock to the experimentally demonstrated Munegumi (2014) route.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs / hydantoin route from oxaloacetate to aspartate
reactions.mechanism: Alpha-ketoacid undergoes cyanide/ammonia/CO2 chemistry through hydantoin and carbamoyl intermediates to the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. (2022) showed alpha-ketoacids including oxaloacetate can yield amino acids through Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin chemistry, with oxaloacetate specifically giving aspartate. This is included as a precursor route only, not as a validated asparagine synthesis.

reactions.name: Cu2+-catalyzed transamination of oxaloacetate to aspartate

conditions.other: rapid but limited yield; oxaloacetate instability is a bottleneck

reactions.mechanism: Metal-assisted transamination of oxaloacetate forms aspartate in a protometabolic amino-transfer network.
reactions.reasoning: Harrison et al. (2023) reported about 5% aspartate yield from Cu2+-catalyzed transamination of oxaloacetate with pyridoxamine. The study also emphasizes oxaloacetate instability, so this reaction is treated as convergent precursor chemistry only.

reactions.name: Fe-promoted protometabolic generation of aspartate from pyruvate-derived network via oxaloacetate

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+/Fe0 protometabolic network with glyoxylate/hydroxylamine inputs implicit
conditions.other: network-level route; oxaloacetate transient and unstable

reactions.mechanism: Iron-driven protometabolic carbon-network chemistry produces oxaloacetate-like intermediates and aspartate from pyruvate-centered feedstocks.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2019) and Springsteen et al. (2018) support Fe-mediated protometabolic networks yielding aspartate from simple carboxylate systems. This network-level step is included to connect hydrothermal pyruvate supply to the aspartate hub while respecting the reported instability of oxaloacetate.

reactions.name: Asparagine deamidation to aspartate

conditions.other: faster at elevated temperature and through repeated wet phases

reactions.mechanism: Thermodynamically favorable hydrolysis of the side-chain amide converts asparagine to aspartate and ammonia.
reactions.reasoning: Kishore, Tewari & Goldberg (2000), Robinson & Robinson (2001), and Bada et al. (1995) show asparagine deamidation is strongly favored and rapid at warm temperatures; this sink is essential to an accurate literature-based network.

reactions.name: Speculative activated amidation of aspartate to asparagine

conditions.catalyst: candidate activating agents such as DAP, cyanamide, COS, or urea not explicitly modeled
conditions.other: dry-phase activation required; no direct experimental demonstration for aspartate to asparagine

reactions.mechanism: Hypothetical activation of side-chain carboxylate followed by ammonia addition to form the amide.
reactions.reasoning: Osumah & Krishnamurthy (2021), Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin (1965), and Leman et al. (2004) discuss prebiotic activating agents, but the deep research report explicitly states that no prebiotically plausible mechanism has yet been demonstrated for selective conversion of aspartate to asparagine. This reaction is retained only as a clearly marked unresolved gap, not as an established route.

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide trapping of asparagine into an asparagine-containing dipeptide surrogate

conditions.catalyst: geothermal wet-dry cycling on mineral interfaces
conditions.other: repeated drying may kinetically trap transient asparagine before deamidation

reactions.mechanism: Condensation/polymerization incorporates transient asparagine into short peptides or oligomers.
reactions.reasoning: Munegumi & Tanikawa (2017) reported that asparagine-containing dipeptides polymerize faster than asparagine alone, supporting kinetic trapping as a possible escape from rapid deamidation, though exact prebiotic activation chemistry remains incomplete.

pathways.name: Direct Sanchez-Orgel cyanoacetylene route
pathways.description: Classical aqueous cyanoacetylene chemistry producing a C4 aminonitrile precursor and then partial hydrolysis to asparagine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic Sutherland route
pathways.description: HCN/H2S/UV chemistry generates cyanoacetylene, maleonitrile, and the identified alpha-aminonitrile precursor to asparagine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge direct atmospheric condensate route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge products contain directly detectable asparagine when analyzed without acid hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal alanine to glow-discharge asparagine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is aminated to alanine, which with formamide under contact glow discharge yields asparagine.

pathways.name: HCN-to-formamide glow-discharge route
pathways.description: Surface HCN is converted to formamide, which together with alanine from hydrothermal chemistry gives asparagine under glow discharge.

pathways.name: Protometabolic pyruvate to aspartate then speculative amidation
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate feeds iron-driven protometabolic networks to aspartate; final amidation is explicitly unresolved and speculative.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs oxaloacetate route then speculative amidation
pathways.description: Oxaloacetate gives aspartate through hydantoin chemistry; conversion to asparagine remains an open problem.

pathways.name: Cu2+ transamination route then speculative amidation
pathways.description: Oxaloacetate is transaminated to aspartate in a protometabolic amino-transfer step; final amidation is unresolved.

pathways.name: Sanchez route with biochemical kinetic trapping
pathways.description: Directly formed asparagine from cyanoacetylene chemistry is rapidly incorporated into a peptide-like product to reduce free-solution deamidation.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge asparagine with biochemical kinetic trapping
pathways.description: Direct spark-generated asparagine is captured into an asparagine-containing peptide surrogate before extensive deamidation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature alignment with the Sanchez-Orgel cyanoacetylene route. The sequence cyanoacetylene -> cyanoacetaldehyde -> aminonitrile precursor -> partial hydrolysis to asparagine is coherent and matches the core historical proposal. Main weakness is the difficult selectivity of partial hydrolysis to amide rather than full hydrolysis to aspartate, which the literature identifies as a major bottleneck.
suggestions: Specify pH and buffering conditions for selective partial hydrolysis
Clarify whether rxn_006 corresponds to a directly demonstrated aminonitrile intermediate or a reconstructed Strecker-like analogue
Note competition with full hydrolysis to aspartate explicitly in pathway definition

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Best-supported route in the network. It closely follows Patel et al. 2015: cyanoacetylene generation from HCN + acetylene under cyanosulfidic photoredox conditions, conversion to maleonitrile and compound-49-like aminonitrile, then hydrolysis to asparagine/aspartate. The only significant uncertainty is again selective partial hydrolysis to the amide.
suggestions: Include explicit Cu photoredox/phosphate-buffered alkaline conditions in the reaction metadata
Distinguish compound 49 analogue from fully assigned structure if exact identity is uncertain
Emphasize that asparagine is likely transient and may require rapid trapping

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway reflects Jiang et al. 2014, where asparagine was detected directly in spark-discharge products without acid hydrolysis. It reaches the target in one step and is literature-grounded. However, as represented in the network it is highly compressed and underspecified mechanistically; the direct transformation HCN + NH3 -> asparagine omits necessary atmospheric/plasma complexity and intermediate chemistry.
suggestions: Represent spark discharge as a condensate-producing atmospheric process rather than a simple stoichiometric bimolecular reaction
Include additional feed gases such as CH4/N2/CO2/H2 where appropriate
Flag the reaction as detection-based rather than mechanistically resolved

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a credible multi-environment path: hydrothermal carbon fixation to pyruvate, reductive amination to alanine, surface generation of formamide, then contact glow discharge formation of asparagine. The alanine -> asparagine endpoint is supported by Munegumi 2014, but the pathway depends on a weakly specified HCN -> formamide surface step and assumes physical convergence of alanine and formamide in a discharge environment.
suggestions: Clarify whether formamide is generated by direct HCN hydration, geochemical concentration, or atmospheric delivery
Specify transport/concentration scenario linking hydrothermal alanine to a volcanic discharge surface setting
Note that rxn_011 is a specialized discharge chemistry rather than general surface reaction

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Similar to P4 and environmentally well connected, but the route remains somewhat artificial because the HCN-to-formamide step is treated as a clean precursor supply and the final contact glow discharge step is not a routine prebiotic pool transformation. Still, the literature does support the endpoint chemistry and the upstream hydrothermal supply of alanine is plausible.
suggestions: Differentiate this pathway from P4 more clearly if they share most chemistry
Add a better-supported formamide generation/concentration step
State the discharge context explicitly as volcanic lightning analogue

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures the literature idea that hydrothermal/protometabolic chemistry can supply aspartate precursors, but it overstates current evidence. The pyruvate-derived oxaloacetate/aspartate network is only partially demonstrated and oxaloacetate instability is a known bottleneck. The terminal amidation of aspartate to asparagine is explicitly speculative and currently unsupported experimentally, making target achievement weak.
suggestions: Replace speculative amidation with experimentally demonstrated direct asparagine-forming routes
Separate oxaloacetate formation from aspartate production more explicitly
Discuss known activators like DAP/cyanamide/COS only as future tests, not effective established reactions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin route to aspartate from oxaloacetate is literature-supported in principle, although the network compresses important intermediates. But as a path to asparagine it fails at the same point as P6: no established prebiotic amidation of aspartate. This makes the pathway useful as precursor chemistry rather than a validated target-reaching route.
suggestions: Include hydantoin or N-carbamoyl aspartate explicitly if retaining this route
Reclassify the pathway as aspartate-generating rather than asparagine-producing unless amidation evidence is added
Avoid counting speculative terminal amidation as equivalent to demonstrated target production

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is the weakest of the protometabolic routes. The cited literature supports Cu2+-catalyzed transamination of oxaloacetate using pyridoxamine, not simple oxaloacetate + ammonia as drawn here. Combined with the unsupported amidation step, the pathway does not currently validate target synthesis.
suggestions: Correct transamination chemistry to include the required amine donor such as pyridoxamine
Acknowledge rapid oxaloacetate decarboxylation explicitly in the pathway design
Do not treat the amidation step as a solved reaction

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a strong extension of the Sanchez-type route because it addresses the central literature problem of asparagine instability by invoking kinetic trapping in peptides. The trapping step is plausible as a conceptual biochemical sink, but the specific dipeptide surrogate remains somewhat generic and should be linked more explicitly to wet-dry peptide chemistry literature.
suggestions: Specify the copartner amino acid and mineral/condensing context for dipeptide formation
Connect peptide trapping to published observations on faster polymerization of asparagine-containing dipeptides
Clarify whether trapping competes effectively with deamidation on the stated timescale

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is attractive because it couples experimentally observed direct asparagine formation in spark discharge products with immediate trapping to mitigate deamidation. Its weakness is not the concept but the simplified representation of spark chemistry and the generic nature of the peptide trapping step.
suggestions: Represent discharge products as a mixture rather than a direct stoichiometric synthesis
Add better-specified wet-dry or mineral-assisted peptide coupling conditions
Discuss whether asparagine survives long enough in condensates for trapping to occur

evaluation.network_feedback:
This is a good, well-connected synthesis network that is notably stronger on literature alignment than many prebiotic networks because it correctly treats prebiotic asparagine as a difficult and unstable target rather than assuming easy amidation from aspartate. Network topology is solid: there are sufficient molecule and reaction nodes, multiple hubs are genuinely reused, convergence around HCN, cyanoacetylene, the aminonitrile precursor, pyruvate, and aspartate is clear, and the graph appears connected. Environmental interconnection is also a strong feature: hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon and alpha-keto-acid precursors, surface cyanosulfidic and discharge chemistry produces direct asparagine-forming routes, and biochemical stages provide trapping/deamidation context.

The main scientific strengths are: strong use of the Sanchez/Ferris/Orgel route, close alignment with Patel et al. cyanosulfidic chemistry, inclusion of Jiang et al. spark-discharge detection, and inclusion of Munegumi's alanine + formamide glow discharge route. The network also correctly encodes asparagine deamidation to aspartate and avoids presenting hydrothermal vents as a net favorable environment for asparagine accumulation.

The principal weaknesses are concentrated in several reactions that compress or overstate evidence. The direct spark-discharge reaction (rxn_010) is detection-based but represented as a simple stoichiometric synthesis; the HCN -> formamide step (rxn_012) is underspecified; the Cu2+-transamination route (rxn_014) omits the actual amine donor from the cited literature; and the protometabolic pyruvate -> oxaloacetate/aspartate step (rxn_015) is too compressed relative to published networks. Most importantly, speculative amidation of aspartate to asparagine (rxn_017) is correctly flagged as speculative but still weakens any pathway that relies on it as a target-reaching step. Those pathways should not be considered equivalent to the demonstrated direct-formation routes.

Overall, this network is chemically and conceptually credible as a mixed-confidence synthesis network for asparagine, with excellent handling of literature-supported direct routes and appropriate acknowledgment of the unsolved amidation problem. It is not approvable at the highest threshold because several target-reaching pathways depend on speculative chemistry and a few reaction representations are overly simplified.
